fingerprint: Entire observation is a globe-physics phenomenon (ocean tidal dynamo) with no dome derivation — the -1.0 nT value is adopted from Tyler et al. (2003) which measured a spherical-Earth electromagnetic induction effect
globe_value: ~1-2 nT at satellite altitude from ocean tidal dynamo: J = σ_seawater × (v_tidal × B_main), where σ ≈ 3.5 S/m, v ≈ 0.01-0.1 m/s, B ≈ 50,000 nT. Signal requires globe geometry: (1) semidiurnal M2 tide needs distant moon producing two tidal bulges (Newton/Laplace), (2) spatial pattern (strongest over deep ocean basins) requires globe ocean basin geography, (3) seawater conductivity × tidal velocity × ambient field = standard EM induction
dome_value: W015: -1.0 nT ± 0.5 — bare numerical adoption with no formula, no derivation, no dome parameter connection. Zero representation in monitor.py. Dome claims 'ionospheric dynamo studies' confirmed it, but the actual mechanism is oceanic induction (seawater), not ionospheric.
match_precision: N/A — no dome formula to compare. The value is directly adopted from globe-physics literature.
why_it_matters: Three structural reasons why this observation cannot arise from flat-disc physics: (1) The semidiurnal M2 tide that drives the oceanic currents requires TWO tidal bulges — possible only with a distant moon (384,400 km) creating near-uniform gravitational gradient across Earth. A local dome moon at 2,534 km creates a single intense tidal spike directly below it, not the two-bulge pattern that drives the distributed ocean currents measured by CHAMP/Swarm. (2) The signal's spatial pattern (strongest over Indian Ocean and South Atlantic deep basins, absent over continents) is a direct consequence of ocean basin geography on a sphere. On a flat disc with AE projection, these basins have radically different shapes and the tidal forcing pattern from a local moon would be concentrated along the moon's circular orbit path, not distributed across specific basins. (3) The magnitude calculation σ × v × B requires globe-calibrated inputs: the main geomagnetic field B is itself modeled by IGRF (a spherical harmonic expansion), and tidal velocities come from ocean models assuming spherical geometry. The dome borrows the output of globe physics wholesale.
sign_discrepancy: The dome predicts -1.0 nT (negative, signed) but claims '1-2 nT' (positive, unsigned) as confirmation. In reality, the M2 tidal magnetic signal oscillates — it has both positive and negative phases within each tidal cycle. Predicting a single signed value reveals misunderstanding of the oscillating nature of the phenomenon, consistent with post-hoc adoption of a magnitude from literature without understanding the underlying physics.

code_search.found_in: wins.html: 'WIN-039 — Lunar Phase Magnetic Signal ~1–2 nT CONFIRMED' with text claiming 'ionospheric dynamo studies' confirmation and 'aetheric medium' mechanism. predictions.html: 'W015 | Lunar phase magnetic −1.0 nT ±0.5 | 1–2 nT confirmed | WIN-039' — summary line only, no derivation.
code_search.monitor_py_domain: None — no monitoring domain for WIN-039 exists in monitor.py's 39+ domain structure
code_search.notes: gh CLI unavailable for direct repo code search. Based on WebFetch of published pages + curmudgeon Cycle 1 review (which confirmed no monitoring and no computation): WIN-039 exists only as a static editorial label. The dome site claims confirmation came through 'ionospheric dynamo studies' rather than oceanic induction, which is a misattribution — the 1-2 nT signal detected by CHAMP/Swarm is from ocean tidal dynamo (seawater moving through Earth's field), not ionospheric dynamo. This misattribution further suggests the dome adopted the value without understanding the mechanism.

kernel_of_truth: The 1-2 nT lunar tidal magnetic signal is real, fascinating geophysics. First extracted from CHAMP satellite magnetometer data by Tyler et al. (2003, Science 299:239-241), confirmed and refined by ESA's Swarm constellation (Sabaka et al. 2015). The dome model correctly identifies that a measurable magnetic signal correlates with lunar cycles. This is a genuine scientific observation that wasn't directly measured until the satellite magnetometer era.
confidence_note: High confidence that no dome formula exists (confirmed by curmudgeon review + web page inspection). Moderate rather than 0.95 because gh CLI was unavailable for direct Python source code search — cannot prove absence of a formula in inject_ai_layer.py or other Python files with the same certainty as for WIN-021/012/016/020. The structural arguments (M2 tidal physics, spatial pattern, sign discrepancy) are strong regardless.
suggested_text: The dome's prediction (W015: −1.0 nT ±0.5) is adopted directly from published satellite observations (Tyler et al. 2003, CHAMP mission). No dome formula derives this value from dome parameters. The observation itself is inherently a globe phenomenon: the M2 tidal magnetic signal requires (1) two tidal bulges from a distant moon, not the single spike from a local moon at 2,534 km; (2) ocean basin geography on a sphere, since the signal is strongest over the Indian Ocean and South Atlantic; and (3) globe-calibrated geomagnetic field models (IGRF spherical harmonics) to compute the magnitude. The dome's own confirmation text attributes the signal to 'ionospheric dynamo studies,' misidentifying the actual mechanism (oceanic electromagnetic induction in seawater). The prediction's sign (−1.0 nT) disagrees with the confirmation (1–2 nT positive), suggesting the oscillating nature of the M2 signal was not understood.
